- [WA] Per-bit objective sum: Optimize each bit layer independently and add the weighted results. This is strongest for xor-looking tasks whose real statement also has addition, subtraction, capacity, lexicographic order, or one witness that must satisfy all bits at once. Harden it with tiny exact active-width islands, high-bit-first and low-bit-first variants, and one global mode such as "carry already relevant"; the missing proof is still cross-bit compatibility.

- [WA] Carry or borrow treated as rare noise: Replace addition/subtraction behavior by xor, parity, or one carry flag, then repair only low bits. It survives carry-free weak tests but fails on long dependencies across the bit where the heuristic committed. A bounded exact suffix, dense low-prefix detector, and separate "no debt" versus "debt active" modes reduce easy breaks without making the quotient state correct.

- [WA] Xor basis overreach: Build a linear basis over `GF(2)` and use rank, maximum xor, or span membership as the answer proxy even when multiplicity, order, cost, construction constraints, or non-xor operators matter. Track duplicate classes, top pivots, and one representative witness per pivot bucket, and brute-force tiny rank; equal rank still does not imply equal structure.

- [WA] Basis canonical witness bias: Output the subset or vector produced by one deterministic pivot order, assuming any span witness is interchangeable. This breaks when the judge cares about length, lexicographic order, side constraints, or compatibility with another graph/array structure. Several pivot orders or cheapest witnesses per pivot harden it while still forgetting many feasible witnesses.

- [WA] Tiny signature quotient: Bucket masks by `popcount`, parity, highest bit, lowest bit, low `k` bits, or a small tuple, then run game logic, DP, or greedy choices on buckets instead of masks. Same-signature supports can have different adjacency, dominance, carry behavior, and futures. Keep a few representatives per bucket and split by top/low support shape; the state merge remains unjustified.

- [WA] Compressed digit or mask DP: Keep only `tight` plus parity, one carry/borrow flag, count of chosen bits, support size, xor, OR, or AND summary. Once states with different future legality merge, counts and optima drift while samples still look coherent. Use exact routing for tiny widths and exact suffixes for the last few bits, but treat the compression as a deliberate wrong quotient.

- [WA] AND/OR closure set only: Maintain only the set of reachable AND or OR values and answer from that frontier, discarding how each value was reached. Equal frontiers can hide different counts, lengths, costs, and compatibility with later choices. Store one short witness, one cheap witness, and capped multiplicity per value if you want the adversarial route to last longer.

- [WA] Greedy highest-bit dominance: Choose pivots, pairs, trie branches, or operations by the highest differing bit and assume lower bits can be fixed later. This fits max-xor-looking objectives but fails when lower-bit feasibility, count, or a global matching/matroid constraint blocks the locally best high bit. Run a low-bit-sensitive variant or bounded residual repair as hardening, not proof.

- [WA] Active-width truncation: Define the width from the current maximum value and ignore bits created by operations, complements, carries, or required output bounds. This is wrong when leading zeroes have semantic weight or later operations expose a higher bit. Prefer the logical width from the statement, and only truncate prefixes that are truly inactive under the problem model.

- [TLE] Full-width SOS transform: Run zeta/Mobius/SOS over the statement width `B`, paying `O(B * 2^B)` even when weak tests only activate a few bits. A legal query over the whole mask universe, a complement condition, or one high active bit makes the width visible from the problem model. Gate the exact transform behind strict `B` thresholds and fall back to sparse summaries that knowingly lose structure.

- [MLE] Full-width mask table: Allocate `2^B` cells for counts, best values, subset closure, visited masks, or transform layers because sample masks are narrow. The memory failure follows from treating every logical mask as a state, not from the container choice. A capped map or coordinate-compressed active set can pass low-width weak tests while turning the strong case into WA.

- [TLE] All-mask submask merging: For every mask, iterate through all strict submasks or partition pairs and rely on the standard submask trick as if it made the loop small. It improves the naive bound but is still `O(3^B)` total, which is fatal when `B` is near the advertised bitmask-DP limit. Keep this route only for tiny `B`, or collapse dense masks to a signature after the cutoff.

- [TLE] Dense single-mask submask scan: Enumerate every submask of each input mask because examples have low popcount, or every supermask/complement submask because examples leave many bits fixed. A high-popcount mask costs `2^popcount(mask)` submasks, while a low-popcount mask has many supermasks in a `B`-bit universe. Cut off expensive masks, sample representatives, or switch to support-size buckets; the cutoff is the intended wrong assumption.

- [TLE/MLE] AND/OR frontier treated as constant: Maintain distinct subarray AND/OR values and assume the per-endpoint frontier is tiny. The documented monotonicity bound is `O(B)` distinct values per start/end, so worst-case work and storage are `O(n * B)` before extra queries, witnesses, or DP dimensions are multiplied in. Cap the frontier or keep only representative values when weak random data collapses faster.

- [MLE] Binary trie node count: Build a 0/1 trie over values, prefixes, versions, or range snapshots assuming shared prefixes will keep it small. A trie is linear in total key length, so `n` unrelated `B`-bit keys or persistent updates can require `Theta(n * B)` nodes. Pool only visible active prefixes, compress single-child chains, or cap versions if the adversarial solution is meant to survive sparse tests.

- [TLE] Basis span enumeration: After constructing a xor basis of rank `r`, enumerate all span values, all basis subsets, or pairs of basis combinations to optimize a non-rank objective. A basis compactly represents `2^r` xor combinations, so full enumeration becomes visible as soon as the statement permits high rank. Limit exact enumeration to tiny `r` and use rank/signature proxies outside it.

- [MLE] Basis span table: Cache answers, witnesses, or costs for every representable xor value in the basis span. This is a `2^r` table even though the basis itself has only `r` vectors, and weak low-rank tests hide the gap. Store one canonical witness per pivot class or a capped sample of span values when construction details are not judged hard.

- [TLE/MLE] Bitmask DP product table: Materialize `dp[mask][last]`, `dp[mask][state]`, or `dp[bit][mask][state]` because each dimension is natural alone. Standard bitmask DP already has `2^n` states, and adding endpoint, automaton, residue, carry, or popcount dimensions gives a visible product-state failure. Roll layers, sparse-store live states, or merge one dimension to a proxy to trade the resource failure for semantic WA.

- [RE] Packed-mask range or shift boundary: Pack a semantic mask or range into one machine word and then shift, index, or slice at a boundary the statement allows: width equal to the word size, zero mask, inclusive endpoint `B`, sign bit included, or negative values. Low positive samples hide it, but boundary-valid inputs can trigger invalid shifts, out-of-range table access, or wrong signed extraction. Use this only when those boundary values are legal in the problem, not as a source-code accident.
